title: Sort stack
description: Sort stack using one extra stack

examples.explanation:
1. Original stack (top first): 5, 1, 4, 2, 3
2. Pop all to array: pop 5 → array=[5], pop 1 → array=[5,1], pop 4 → array=[5,1,4], pop 2 → array=[5,1,4,2], pop 3 → array=[5,1,4,2,3]
3. Sort array: quicksort partitions → [1,2,3,4,5]
4. Push back to stack (reverse order): push 5 → stack=[5], push 4 → stack=[4,5], push 3 → stack=[3,4,5], push 2 → stack=[2,3,4,5], push 1 → stack=[1,2,3,4,5]
5. Final stack (top first): 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n) with Extra Array
approaches.summary: Pop all elements from the stack into an array, sort the array using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort, then push sorted elements back onto the stack. This approach leverages external sorting but violates the constraint of using only one extra stack.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Array Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of sorting a stack by transferring to array, sorting, and rebuilding
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the stack sorting problem by leveraging external data structures, specifically an array, to perform efficient sorting. The intuition is simple: since stacks have LIFO access which makes in-place sorting complex, we can temporarily move all elements to an array where we have random access and can apply standard sorting algorithms. The key insight is that this transformation allows us to use O(n log n) sorting algorithms instead of the O(n²) approaches typically required with stack constraints. The algorithm proceeds in three clear phases: first, we pop all elements from the original stack into an array, which gives us linear access to all values. Second, we sort this array using an efficient algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. Finally, we push the sorted elements back onto the stack, ensuring the smallest element ends up at the bottom (or top, depending on desired order). The time complexity is dominated by the sorting step at O(n log n), while space complexity is O(n) for the auxiliary array. This approach violates the typical constraint of using only one extra stack, but demonstrates how problem constraints dramatically affect solution strategies. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty stacks, incorrectly managing stack pointers during transfer, or not considering whether the sorted stack should have ascending or descending order. The connection to real-world patterns is evident in scenarios where temporary buffers are used to overcome data structure limitations before restoring the original structure.

examples.trace:
1. Original stack (top first): 5, 1, 4, 2, 3
2. Pop all to array: pop 5 → array=[5], pop 1 → array=[5,1], pop 4 → array=[5,1,4], pop 2 → array=[5,1,4,2], pop 3 → array=[5,1,4,2,3]
3. Sort array: quicksort partitions → [1,2,3,4,5]
4. Push back to stack (reverse order): push 5 → stack=[5], push 4 → stack=[4,5], push 3 → stack=[3,4,5], push 2 → stack=[2,3,4,5], push 1 → stack=[1,2,3,4,5]
5. Final stack (top first): 1,2,3,4,5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Array Sorting with Quicksort
ladders.desc: Implement quicksort algorithm to sort the extracted array
ladders.explanation: The quicksort algorithm is the computational heart of this approach, providing the O(n log n) efficiency that makes the brute force method viable. The intuition behind quicksort is divide-and-conquer: select a pivot element, partition the array so all elements less than the pivot come before it and all greater elements come after, then recursively sort the sub-arrays. This works because each partitioning step places the pivot in its final sorted position, gradually reducing the problem size. The algorithm begins by checking if the array has 0 or 1 elements (base case). For larger arrays, we select a pivot (commonly the last element), then iterate through the array, swapping elements to create two partitions. The partitioning process uses two pointers: one tracks the boundary between elements less than the pivot and those greater, while another scans through the array. After partitioning, we recursively apply quicksort to the left (smaller elements) and right (larger elements) partitions. Time complexity averages O(n log n) but degrades to O(n²) in worst-case scenarios like already sorted arrays with poor pivot selection. Space complexity is O(log n) for the recursion stack. This sub-routine connects directly to L0 as it's the sorting engine that operates on the extracted array. Common mistakes include incorrect pivot selection, off-by-one errors in partition indices, and not handling duplicate values properly. The recursive nature requires careful base case handling to avoid infinite recursion.

examples.trace:
1. Initial: [5,1,4,2,3], pivot=3
2. Partition: i=-1, j=0: 5>3 → no swap, j=1: 1<3 → swap arr[0] and arr[1] → [1,5,4,2,3], i=0, j=2: 4>3 → no swap, j=3: 2<3 → swap arr[1] and arr[3] → [1,2,4,5,3], i=1, j=4: pivot swap arr[2] and arr[4] → [1,2,3,5,4]
3. Recursively sort left [1,2]: base case sorted
4. Recursively sort right [5,4]: pivot=4, partition → [4,5]
5. Final: [1,2,3,4,5]

1. Initial: [10,-5,0,7,-2], pivot=-2
2. Partition: i=-1, j=0: 10>-2 → no swap, j=1: -5<-2 → swap arr[0] and arr[1] → [-5,10,0,7,-2], i=0, j=2: 0>-2 → no swap, j=3: 7>-2 → no swap, j=4: pivot swap arr[1] and arr[4] → [-5,-2,0,7,10]
3. Left [-5]: base case
4. Right [0,7,10]: pivot=10, partition → [0,7,10]
5. Final: [-5,-2,0,7,10]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Transfer Between Stack and Array
ladders.desc: Move all elements between stack and array while preserving order relationships
ladders.explanation: This core logic handles the bidirectional transfer of elements between the stack (LIFO structure) and array (random access structure). The intuition is that we need to overcome the stack's access limitations by temporarily storing elements in an array, but we must ensure no data is lost and the final stack has the correct sorted order. When transferring from stack to array, we repeatedly pop from the stack until it's empty, appending each popped element to the array. This results in the array containing elements in reverse order compared to the original stack (since popping gives us the top element first). After sorting the array, we push elements back onto the stack. If we want ascending order with smallest at the bottom, we push from the end of the sorted array; if we want smallest at the top, we push from the beginning. The key insight is that the transfer process is linear O(n) in both directions, making it efficient compared to the sorting complexity. This logic connects to L1 as it provides the array that gets sorted, and to L0 as it's the wrapper that coordinates the overall solution. Common mistakes include not clearing the stack completely before pushing back, pushing in the wrong direction for desired order, or modifying the array while transferring. The transfer must be complete and atomic—we shouldn't interleave popping and pushing operations.

examples.input: Array to stack (ascending, smallest at bottom): array=[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
examples.trace:
1. Start: array=[1,2,3,4,5], stack=[]
2. Push from start: push(1) → stack=[1]
3. push(2) → stack=[2,1]
4. push(3) → stack=[3,2,1]
5. push(4) → stack=[4,3,2,1]
6. push(5) → stack=[5,4,3,2,1]
7. Final stack top to bottom: 5,4,3,2,1

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Partitioning for Quicksort
ladders.desc: Partition an array around a pivot element for the quicksort algorithm
ladders.explanation: Partitioning is the fundamental operation that makes quicksort efficient. The intuition is to rearrange the array so that all elements less than a chosen pivot come before it, and all elements greater come after, with the pivot ending up in its final sorted position. This works because once partitioned, we know the pivot is in the correct position for the fully sorted array, and we can recursively sort the sub-arrays independently. The algorithm selects a pivot (typically the last element), then uses two indices: i tracks the boundary between elements less than the pivot and those greater, starting at -1; j scans through the array from start to pivot-1. For each element at position j, if it's less than the pivot, we increment i and swap elements at i and j. This gradually builds the 'less than' region at the beginning of the array. After the scan, we place the pivot in its correct position by swapping it with the element at i+1. The time complexity is O(n) for a single partition, and space is O(1) aside from the array itself. This building block connects to L2 as it's the key step in sorting the extracted array, and to L1 as it's the core of quicksort. Common mistakes include incorrect pivot selection, off-by-one errors in index management, not handling equal elements properly, and modifying the pivot during partitioning. The partition must be stable in terms of final pivot position but doesn't need to preserve the original order of equal elements.

examples.trace:
1. Initial: [5,1,4,2,3], pivot=3, i=-1
2. j=0: 5>3 → no swap, array unchanged
3. j=1: 1<3 → i=0, swap arr[0] and arr[1] → [1,5,4,2,3]
4. j=2: 4>3 → no swap
5. j=3: 2<3 → i=1, swap arr[1] and arr[3] → [1,2,4,5,3]
6. End of loop: swap pivot arr[4] with arr[i+1=2] → [1,2,3,5,4]
7. Pivot now at index 2

1. Initial: [10,-5,0,7,-2], pivot=-2, i=-1
2. j=0: 10>-2 → no swap
3. j=1: -5<-2 → i=0, swap arr[0] and arr[1] → [-5,10,0,7,-2]
4. j=2: 0>-2 → no swap
5. j=3: 7>-2 → no swap
6. End: swap pivot arr[4] with arr[i+1=1] → [-5,-2,0,7,10]
7. Pivot at index 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations: adding and removing elements from the top
ladders.explanation: Push and pop are the essential operations that define stack behavior. The intuition is LIFO (Last-In-First-Out): the most recently added element is always the first one removed. Push adds an element to the top of the stack, increasing its size. Pop removes and returns the top element, decreasing the stack size. These operations work because they maintain a single point of access (the top), making stacks simple but limited in functionality. In our sorting approach, we use pop to extract all elements from the original stack (emptying it completely), and push to rebuild the sorted stack. The time complexity for both operations is O(1) assuming array-based stack implementation, as they only involve updating a top pointer or index. Space complexity is O(1) per operation plus the underlying storage. This basic operation connects to L3 as it's how we transfer elements to/from the array, and ultimately to L0 as the interface to the stack data structure. Common mistakes include popping from an empty stack (which should throw an error or return a sentinel value), not updating the stack size correctly, or confusing stack order (top vs bottom). In many implementations, stacks also support peek (view top without removing) and isEmpty checks, which are useful for control flow.

examples.input: Initial stack: [5, 1, 4] (top to bottom), operations: pop(), push(2), pop(), push(3)
examples.trace:
1. Start: stack=[5,1,4] (top=5)
2. pop() → returns 5, stack=[1,4]
3. push(2) → stack=[2,1,4]
4. pop() → returns 2, stack=[1,4]
5. push(3) → stack=[3,1,4]
6. Final: top to bottom: 3,1,4

examples.input: Empty stack, operations: push(10), push(20), pop(), push(30)
examples.trace:
1. Start: stack=[]
2. push(10) → stack=[10]
3. push(20) → stack=[20,10]
4. pop() → returns 20, stack=[10]
5. push(30) → stack=[30,10]
6. Final: top to bottom: 30,10

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Fundamental array concepts: creation, access, and modification by index
ladders.explanation: Arrays are the most basic data structure, providing contiguous memory locations that can be accessed in constant time using indices. The intuition is that an array is like a numbered series of boxes where each box holds one value, and we can directly jump to any box if we know its position (index). This random access capability is what makes arrays powerful for sorting algorithms like quicksort. Arrays typically use 0-based indexing, meaning the first element is at position 0, the second at position 1, etc. Basic operations include: creating an array of a certain size, accessing an element at a specific index (which is O(1) time), modifying an element at an index, and iterating through all elements. In our stack sorting approach, we use an array as temporary storage because it allows us to sort efficiently—something that's difficult with stack's LIFO access. The space complexity for an array is O(n) where n is the number of elements. This foundation connects to L4 as arrays are the alternative structure we use alongside stacks, and to all higher levels as the medium for sorting. Common beginner mistakes include accessing indices outside the array bounds (causing errors), confusing 0-based with 1-based indexing, and not understanding that arrays have fixed size in some languages (requiring resizing or using dynamic arrays).

examples.input: Create array [5, 1, 4], access index 1, modify index 2 to 9
examples.trace:
1. Create array: arr = [5, 1, 4]
2. Access index 1: arr[1] = 1
3. Modify index 2: arr[2] = 9
4. Array now: [5, 1, 9]
5. Indices: 0→5, 1→1, 2→9

examples.input: Empty array, append 10, append 20, access index 0
examples.trace:
1. Start: arr = []
2. Append 10: arr = [10]
3. Append 20: arr = [10, 20]
4. Access index 0: arr[0] = 10
5. Current indices: 0→10, 1→20

testCases.input: Array: [0, 0, 0], access index 1, modify index 2 to 5
Array: [-3, 5, -1], access index 0, modify index 1 to 0
Array: [7, 3, 9], access index 2, modify index 0 to 1
Array: [6, 4, 8], modify index 1 to 2, access index 1

approaches.name: Two-Stack Simulated Sort O(n²)
approaches.summary: Use the extra stack as a temporary sorted buffer: repeatedly pop from the original stack, insert each element into the extra stack in sorted order by shifting elements back and forth, then transfer all elements back to the original stack. This mimics insertion sort using two stacks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Two-Stack Simulated Insertion Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to sort a stack in ascending order using only one extra stack and stack operations.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a stack in ascending order (smallest at top) using only one additional stack and standard stack operations (push, pop, peek/isEmpty). The Two-Stack Simulated Sort approach mimics insertion sort by using the extra stack as a temporary sorted buffer. The intuition is to repeatedly pop elements from the original stack, insert each into the extra stack in correct sorted position by shifting elements back and forth, then finally transfer all sorted elements back to the original stack. This works because we maintain the extra stack in sorted order (ascending from top) throughout the process. The algorithm: 1) While original stack is not empty, pop element 'temp'. 2) While extra stack is not empty AND top of extra stack > temp, pop from extra stack and push to original stack. 3) Push 'temp' to extra stack. 4) Repeat until original stack empty. 5) Transfer all elements from extra stack back to original stack. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case as each element may require shifting all previously sorted elements. Space complexity is O(n) for the extra stack. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete sortStack function that calls the insertInSortedOrder helper. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison direction (should be > for ascending), forgetting to transfer back to original stack, and infinite loops from improper while conditions. Real code pattern: def sortStack(stack): sorted_stack = [] while stack: temp = stack.pop() while sorted_stack and sorted_stack[-1] > temp: stack.append(sorted_stack.pop()) sorted_stack.append(temp) while sorted_stack: stack.append(sorted_stack.pop()) return stack

examples.trace: Original: [3,1,4,2] (top=3). Step1: pop 3, sorted_stack=[], push 3 → sorted_stack=[3]. Step2: pop 1, sorted_stack=[3], 3>1 so pop 3 to original, push 1 → sorted_stack=[1], push 3 back → sorted_stack=[1,3]. Step3: pop 4, sorted_stack=[1,3], 3<4 so push 4 → sorted_stack=[1,3,4]. Step4: pop 2, sorted_stack=[1,3,4], 4>2 so pop 4 to original, 3>2 so pop 3 to original, 1<2 so push 2 → sorted_stack=[1,2], push 3 back → sorted_stack=[1,2,3], push 4 back → sorted_stack=[1,2,3,4]. Transfer all back: original=[1,2,3,4].

Original: [5,-2,0,8,3] (top=5). Step1: pop 5 → sorted_stack=[5]. Step2: pop -2, 5>-2 so pop 5 to original, push -2 → sorted_stack=[-2], push 5 back → sorted_stack=[-2,5]. Step3: pop 0, 5>0 so pop 5 to original, -2<0 so push 0 → sorted_stack=[-2,0], push 5 back → sorted_stack=[-2,0,5]. Step4: pop 8, 5<8 so push 8 → sorted_stack=[-2,0,5,8]. Step5: pop 3, 8>3 so pop 8 to original, 5>3 so pop 5 to original, 0<3 so push 3 → sorted_stack=[-2,0,3], push 5 back → sorted_stack=[-2,0,3,5], push 8 back → sorted_stack=[-2,0,3,5,8]. Transfer back: original=[-2,0,3,5,8].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Sorted Stack
ladders.desc: Helper function to insert a single element into a sorted stack while maintaining sorted order.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the sorting algorithm. Given a sorted stack (ascending from top) and an element to insert, the function inserts the element at the correct position while preserving the sorted order. The intuition is similar to insertion in insertion sort: we temporarily remove elements from the sorted stack that are larger than our element, insert the element, then put the removed elements back. This works because the sorted stack invariant ensures we only need to compare with top elements. Algorithm: 1) While sorted stack is not empty AND top of sorted stack > element to insert, pop from sorted stack and push to a temporary stack (original stack in full problem). 2) Push element to sorted stack. 3) While temporary stack is not empty, pop and push back to sorted stack. Time complexity is O(k) where k is number of elements larger than insertion element. Space complexity is O(k) for temporary storage. This connects to L0 as the repeated application of this subroutine sorts the entire stack. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operator (should be > for ascending), not handling empty sorted stack case, and forgetting to restore elements from temporary stack. Real code pattern: def insertSorted(sorted_stack, element): temp_stack = [] while sorted_stack and sorted_stack[-1] > element: temp_stack.append(sorted_stack.pop()) sorted_stack.append(element) while temp_stack: sorted_stack.append(temp_stack.pop()) return sorted_stack

examples.trace: Sorted stack: [2,4,6] (top=2). Step1: 2<3, no pop. Step2: push 3 → [2,3,4,6]. No temp elements to restore. Result: [2,3,4,6] (top=2).

Sorted stack: [1,5,9] (top=1). Step1: 1<7, no pop. Step2: 5<7, no pop. Step3: 9>7 so pop 9 to temp → sorted=[1,5], temp=[9]. Step4: push 7 → sorted=[1,5,7]. Step5: restore 9 from temp → sorted=[1,5,7,9]. Result: [1,5,7,9] (top=1).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Sorted Invariant While Shifting Between Stacks
ladders.desc: Technique of temporarily moving elements between two stacks to maintain sorted order during insertion.
ladders.explanation: This core logic demonstrates how to insert an element into a sorted stack by temporarily moving larger elements to another stack. The intuition is that a stack's LIFO property restricts access to only the top element, so to insert an element in the middle, we must temporarily remove elements above the insertion point. By using a second stack as temporary storage, we can 'peek' at elements deeper in the stack. The technique: 1) Compare element with top of sorted stack. 2) If top is larger, pop it to temporary stack and repeat. 3) Once top is smaller or stack empty, push element. 4) Restore all elements from temporary stack back to sorted stack. This maintains the sorted invariant because we only remove elements larger than our insertion element, and restoring them preserves order. Time complexity depends on number of larger elements shifted. Space complexity is O(m) for temporary storage. This connects to L1 as the mechanism used in the insertSorted function. Common mistakes include not checking stack emptiness before peek, incorrect comparison logic, and failing to restore all temporary elements. Real code pattern: while sorted_stack and sorted_stack[-1] > element: temp_stack.append(sorted_stack.pop()) sorted_stack.append(element) while temp_stack: sorted_stack.append(temp_stack.pop())

examples.trace: Start: sorted=[1,3,5], temp=[]. Step1: top=1, 1<2 so no pop. Step2: push 2 → sorted=[1,2,3,5]. No temp elements to restore. Result: sorted=[1,2,3,5].

Start: sorted=[4,6,8], temp=[]. Step1: top=4, 4<5 so no pop. Step2: top=6, 6>5 so pop 6 to temp → sorted=[4], temp=[6]. Step3: push 5 → sorted=[4,5]. Step4: restore 6 from temp → sorted=[4,5,6]. Step5: restore 8 (was below 6) → sorted=[4,5,6,8]. Result: sorted=[4,5,6,8].

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare and Conditionally Move Stack Elements
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of comparing top stack element with a value and moving it based on condition.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental operation of peeking at the top of a stack, comparing it with a value, and conditionally popping it to another stack. The intuition is that stacks only allow access to the top element, so to examine or manipulate elements deeper in the stack, we must temporarily remove top elements. This is essential for insertion into sorted stacks because we need to find the correct position by comparing with multiple elements. The technique: 1) Check if stack is empty. 2) If not empty, peek at top element. 3) Compare top with target value using a condition (e.g., greater than). 4) If condition true, pop from current stack and push to another stack. 5) Repeat until condition false or stack empty. Time complexity is O(k) for k elements moved. Space complexity is O(1) aside from stack storage. This connects to L2 as the loop that shifts elements during insertion. Common mistakes include not checking emptiness before peek, infinite loops from incorrect conditions, and losing elements by not storing them properly. Real code pattern: while stack and stack[-1] > value: other_stack.append(stack.pop())

examples.trace: Start: stack=[3,1,4] (top=3), other=[]. Step1: stack not empty, top=3, 3>2 true → pop 3 to other → stack=[1,4], other=[3]. Step2: stack not empty, top=1, 1>2 false → stop. Result: stack=[1,4], other=[3].
Start: stack=[5,2,7] (top=5), other=[]. Step1: top=5, 5>4 true → pop 5 to other → stack=[2,7], other=[5]. Step2: top=2, 2>4 false → stop. Result: stack=[2,7], other=[5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Transfer All Elements Between Stacks
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of moving all elements from one stack to another using push/pop.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves transferring every element from a source stack to a destination stack using only push and pop operations. The intuition is simple: repeatedly pop from source and push to destination until source is empty. This is essential in stack algorithms for rearranging elements or using a stack as temporary storage. The algorithm: 1) While source stack is not empty, pop an element from source. 2) Push that element to destination stack. 3) Repeat until source empty. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of elements transferred. Space complexity is O(1) aside from stack storage. Note that order reverses if transferring directly (LIFO property), but in our sorting algorithm, we often transfer back and forth to maintain order. This connects to L3 as part of restoring elements after conditional moves. Common mistakes include infinite loops from not updating stack state, losing elements by not storing popped values, and incorrect termination conditions. Real code pattern: while source_stack: destination_stack.append(source_stack.pop())

examples.trace: Start: source=[1,2,3], dest=[]. Step1: pop 3 from source → source=[1,2], push 3 to dest → dest=[3]. Step2: pop 2 → source=[1], push 2 → dest=[3,2]. Step3: pop 1 → source=[], push 1 → dest=[3,2,1]. Result: source=[], dest=[3,2,1].
Start: source=[5], dest=[0]. Step1: pop 5 from source → source=[], push 5 to dest → dest=[0,5]. Result: source=[], dest=[0,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Push and Pop Operations
ladders.desc: Prerequisite understanding of basic stack operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top).
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the fundamental stack operations that every stack algorithm builds upon. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure where elements are added and removed from the top only. Push adds an element to the top of the stack. Pop removes and returns the top element. The intuition is like a stack of plates: you add plates to the top and remove from the top. These operations are essential because they define how we access and manipulate data in stacks. In Python lists used as stacks, push is append() and pop is pop(). Time complexity for both is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) per operation. This connects to L4 as the building blocks for transferring elements. Common mistakes include popping from empty stack (causes error), confusing top with bottom, and misunderstanding LIFO order. Real code pattern: stack = [] # initialize empty stack; stack.append(5) # push 5; top = stack.pop() # pop and get top element.

examples.input: Start: stack=[], operations: push 3, push 1, pop, push 4
examples.trace: Step1: push 3 → stack=[3]. Step2: push 1 → stack=[3,1]. Step3: pop → removes 1, stack=[3]. Step4: push 4 → stack=[3,4]. Result: stack=[3,4].

examples.input: Start: stack=[2,5], operations: pop, push 7, pop
examples.trace: Step1: pop → removes 5, stack=[2]. Step2: push 7 → stack=[2,7]. Step3: pop → removes 7, stack=[2]. Result: stack=[2].

testCases.input: stack=[], operations: push a, push b

approaches.name: Optimized Two-Stack Merge Sort O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Implement a merge sort variant using the two stacks: recursively split the stack into halves using temporary pops, sort each half via recursion, and merge them back in sorted order using the extra stack. This reduces time complexity by leveraging divide-and-conquer principles.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Two-Stack Merge Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of merge sort variant using two stacks to sort a stack in O(n log n) time.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a stack using only one additional stack as temporary storage, with no other data structures allowed. The optimized two-stack merge sort approach applies divide-and-conquer principles to achieve O(n log n) time complexity, which is optimal for comparison-based sorting with stack constraints. The intuition is to treat the stack as an array that can be recursively split into halves, sorted independently, and then merged back together using the extra stack as a workspace. The key insight is that we can simulate array-like indexing on a stack by carefully managing temporary pops and pushes. The algorithm works by: 1) Recursively splitting the original stack into two halves by popping elements into temporary storage and counting, 2) Sorting each half via recursion, 3) Merging the sorted halves back together using the extra stack to compare and combine elements in order. The time complexity is O(n log n) because we perform O(log n) levels of recursion, and at each level we process O(n) elements during splitting and merging. Space complexity is O(n) for the extra stack and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the merge subroutine from L1 with the recursive splitting logic. Common mistakes include: not properly counting elements during splitting (leading to incorrect halves), forgetting to handle base cases (empty or single-element stacks), and incorrectly managing the temporary storage during merging (elements can get lost). Real code pattern: def sort_stack(stack): if len(stack) <= 1: return; mid = len(stack)//2; left = split_first_half(stack, mid); right = stack; sort_stack(left); sort_stack(right); merge_stacks(left, right, stack).

examples.trace: Step 1: Original stack: [5,1,4,2,3] (top=5). Step 2: Split into halves: left=[5,1] (top=5), right=[4,2,3] (top=4). Step 3: Recursively sort left: split [5,1] → [5] and [1], merge → [1,5]. Step 4: Recursively sort right: split [4,2,3] → [4] and [2,3], sort [2,3] → [2,3], merge [4] and [2,3] → [2,3,4]. Step 5: Merge left=[1,5] and right=[2,3,4]: compare 1 and 2 → push 1 to result, compare 5 and 2 → push 2, compare 5 and 3 → push 3, compare 5 and 4 → push 4, push remaining 5 → result=[1,2,3,4,5]. Step 6: Final sorted stack: [1,2,3,4,5] (top=1).

Step 1: Original stack: [10,-5,0,7,3] (top=10). Step 2: Split: left=[10,-5] (top=10), right=[0,7,3] (top=0). Step 3: Sort left: split [10,-5] → [10] and [-5], merge → [-5,10]. Step 4: Sort right: split [0,7,3] → [0] and [7,3], sort [7,3] → [3,7], merge [0] and [3,7] → [0,3,7]. Step 5: Merge left=[-5,10] and right=[0,3,7]: compare -5 and 0 → push -5, compare 10 and 0 → push 0, compare 10 and 3 → push 3, compare 10 and 7 → push 7, push remaining 10 → result=[-5,0,3,7,10]. Step 6: Final sorted stack: [-5,0,3,7,10] (top=-5).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Stacks
ladders.desc: Merge two sorted stacks into one sorted stack using an extra temporary stack.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the merge sort algorithm, responsible for combining two already-sorted stacks into a single sorted stack. The intuition is similar to merging two sorted arrays, but adapted for stack operations where we can only access the top element. We use the extra stack as temporary storage to compare and transfer elements in sorted order. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty result stack. 2) While both input stacks are non-empty: compare their top elements, pop the smaller one and push it to the extra stack. 3) Push any remaining elements from the non-empty stack to the extra stack. 4) Reverse the extra stack back to the result stack to maintain correct order (since stacks are LIFO). Time complexity is O(n+m) where n and m are sizes of the two stacks, as each element is processed once. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the extra stack. Connection to L0: This merge subroutine is called after recursively sorting halves in the full solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to reverse the extra stack (resulting in descending order), incorrectly comparing elements when one stack is empty, and not properly handling duplicate values. Real code pattern: def merge_stacks(stack1, stack2, result): temp = []; while stack1 and stack2: if stack1[-1] <= stack2[-1]: temp.append(stack1.pop()); else: temp.append(stack2.pop()); while stack1: temp.append(stack1.pop()); while stack2: temp.append(stack2.pop()); while temp: result.append(temp.pop()).

examples.trace: Step 1: stack1=[1,5], stack2=[2,3,4], temp=[]. Step 2: Compare tops 1 and 2 → pop 1 to temp: temp=[1], stack1=[5]. Step 3: Compare 5 and 2 → pop 2 to temp: temp=[1,2], stack2=[3,4]. Step 4: Compare 5 and 3 → pop 3: temp=[1,2,3], stack2=[4]. Step 5: Compare 5 and 4 → pop 4: temp=[1,2,3,4], stack2=[]. Step 6: stack2 empty, pop remaining 5: temp=[1,2,3,4,5], stack1=[]. Step 7: Move temp to result: pop 5 → result=[5], pop 4 → result=[5,4], pop 3 → result=[5,4,3], pop 2 → result=[5,4,3,2], pop 1 → result=[5,4,3,2,1]. Step 8: Reverse once more for ascending top order: final=[1,2,3,4,5] (top=1).

Step 1: stack1=[-5,10], stack2=[0,7], temp=[]. Step 2: Compare -5 and 0 → pop -5: temp=[-5], stack1=[10]. Step 3: Compare 10 and 0 → pop 0: temp=[-5,0], stack2=[7]. Step 4: Compare 10 and 7 → pop 7: temp=[-5,0,7], stack2=[]. Step 5: stack2 empty, pop 10: temp=[-5,0,7,10], stack1=[]. Step 6: Move temp to result: pop 10 → result=[10], pop 7 → result=[10,7], pop 0 → result=[10,7,0], pop -5 → result=[10,7,0,-5]. Step 7: Reverse once more for ascending top order: final=[-5,0,7,10] (top=-5).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Splitting of Stack into Halves
ladders.desc: Divide a stack into two approximately equal halves using temporary storage and recursion.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the 'divide' part of divide-and-conquer by splitting a stack into two halves, which is essential for merge sort. The intuition is that we cannot directly index a stack, but we can simulate splitting by popping elements, counting them, and using temporary storage to reconstruct halves. The algorithm: 1) If stack size ≤ 1, return it as is (base case). 2) Otherwise, pop all elements to a temporary list to count size n. 3) Calculate mid = n//2. 4) Push first mid elements back to form the left half stack. 5) Push remaining elements to form the right half stack. 6) Recursively apply the same splitting to each half until base case is reached. Time complexity is O(n) per split level, contributing to O(n log n) overall. Space complexity is O(n) for temporary storage. Connection to L1: After splitting, each half is sorted (via recursion) and then merged using the L1 subroutine. Common mistakes: incorrect calculation of mid (off-by-one errors), not properly restoring elements to the correct halves, and infinite recursion if base case is missing. Real code pattern: def split_stack(stack): if len(stack) <= 1: return stack; temp = []; while stack: temp.append(stack.pop()); n = len(temp); mid = n//2; left = []; for i in range(mid): left.append(temp.pop()); right = []; while temp: right.append(temp.pop()); return left, right.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original stack: [5,1,4,2,3]. Step 2: Pop all to temp: temp=[3,2,4,1,5] (from top). Step 3: Count n=5, mid=2. Step 4: Pop first 2 from temp to left: pop 5 → left=[5], pop 1 → left=[5,1]; temp now=[3,2,4]. Step 5: Pop remaining from temp to right: pop 4 → right=[4], pop 2 → right=[4,2], pop 3 → right=[4,2,3]. Step 6: Left half stack: [5,1] (top=5), right half: [4,2,3] (top=4).

Step 1: Original stack: [10,-5,0,7]. Step 2: Pop to temp: temp=[7,0,-5,10]. Step 3: n=4, mid=2. Step 4: Pop first 2: pop 10 → left=[10], pop -5 → left=[10,-5]; temp=[7,0]. Step 5: Pop remaining: pop 0 → right=[0], pop 7 → right=[0,7]. Step 6: Left: [10,-5] (top=10), right: [0,7] (top=0).

ladders.title: Building Block — Reverse a Stack Using Another Stack
ladders.desc: Reverse the order of elements in a stack using one additional stack.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial for the merge step, where we need to reverse a temporary stack to maintain sorted order. The intuition is that stacks are LIFO (Last-In-First-Out), so to reverse, we can transfer all elements to another stack, which naturally inverts the order. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty temporary stack. 2) While original stack is not empty: pop an element and push it to the temporary stack. 3) The temporary stack now contains elements in reverse order. 4) To restore to original stack (if needed), pop from temporary and push back. Time complexity is O(n) as each element is moved twice. Space complexity is O(n) for the extra stack. Connection to L2: This reversal is used after merging in L1 to correct the order before returning the result. Common mistakes: not handling empty stacks, or incorrectly assuming the reversal happens in-place without an extra stack. Real code pattern: def reverse_stack(stack): temp = []; while stack: temp.append(stack.pop()); return temp.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original stack: [1,2,3]. Step 2: Pop 1 → temp=[1], stack=[2,3]. Step 3: Pop 2 → temp=[1,2], stack=[3]. Step 4: Pop 3 → temp=[1,2,3], stack=[]. Step 5: Temp stack: [3,2,1] (top=3).

Step 1: Original: [5,4,3,2,1]. Step 2: Pop 5 → temp=[5], stack=[4,3,2,1]. Step 3: Pop 4 → temp=[5,4], stack=[3,2,1]. Step 4: Pop 3 → temp=[5,4,3], stack=[2,1]. Step 5: Pop 2 → temp=[5,4,3,2], stack=[1]. Step 6: Pop 1 → temp=[5,4,3,2,1], stack=[]. Step 7: Temp: [1,2,3,4,5] (top=1).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Transfer Elements Between Stacks
ladders.desc: Move elements from one stack to another using push and pop operations.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the building block for all stack manipulations in the algorithm. The intuition is that stacks only allow access to the top element, so to transfer, we repeatedly pop from the source and push to the destination. This is a core pattern in stack-based algorithms. The algorithm: 1) While source stack is not empty: pop the top element and push it to the destination stack. 2) This transfers all elements, reversing their order in the process (since LIFO). Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements transferred. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the stacks. Connection to L3: This operation is used in L3 to reverse a stack by transferring to a temporary stack and back. Common mistakes: not checking if the source stack is empty before popping, or losing elements if not stored properly. Real code pattern: def transfer(source, dest): while source: dest.append(source.pop()).

examples.trace: Step 1: Source=[1,2,3], dest=[]. Step 2: Pop 1 from source → dest=[1], source=[2,3]. Step 3: Pop 2 → dest=[1,2], source=[3]. Step 4: Pop 3 → dest=[1,2,3], source=[]. Step 5: Final: source=[], dest=[3,2,1] (top=3).
Step 1: Source=[5,4], dest=[0]. Step 2: Pop 5 → dest=[0,5], source=[4]. Step 3: Pop 4 → dest=[0,5,4], source=[]. Step 4: Final: source=[], dest=[0,4,5] (top=0).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Operations: Push and Pop
ladders.desc: Understand basic stack operations and LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) principle.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept, essential for anyone new to stacks. A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO principle: the last element added is the first one removed. The two fundamental operations are push (add an element to the top) and pop (remove the top element). Intuition: Think of a stack of plates—you add plates to the top and remove from the top. This behavior makes stacks useful for problems involving reversal, recursion simulation, and temporary storage. In programming, stacks are often implemented using lists or arrays with append (push) and pop methods. Time complexity for push and pop is O(1) amortized. Space complexity depends on the number of elements stored. Connection to L4: These basic operations are used in L4 to transfer elements between stacks. Common mistakes: trying to access elements other than the top without popping, or misunderstanding that pop removes and returns the top element. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(1) (push); top = stack.pop() (pop).

examples.input: Start with empty stack, push 1, push 2, pop, push 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Start: []. Step 2: Push 1 → stack=[1]. Step 3: Push 2 → stack=[1,2]. Step 4: Pop → removes 2, stack=[1]. Step 5: Push 3 → stack=[1,3]. Final stack: [1,3] (top=3).

examples.input: Start with stack [5, 4], pop, push 6, pop
examples.trace: Step 1: Start: [5,4]. Step 2: Pop → removes 4, stack=[5]. Step 3: Push 6 → stack=[5,6]. Step 4: Pop → removes 6, stack=[5]. Final stack: [5] (top=5).
